dtcs.name: Random/Multiple Cylinder Misfire Detected
dtcs.description: The PCM has detected misfires occurring on multiple cylinders with no single cylinder dominating. Often accompanied by a flashing check engine light under load.
common_causes: Worn spark plugs or coil boots

real_fixes.fix: Replace all spark plugs and inspect coil boots
Replace fuel pump or fuel pressure regulator after confirming low rail pressure
Smoke-test and repair intake vacuum leak

dtcs.tech_notes: A flashing MIL means active catalyst-damaging misfire. Pull misfire counters by cylinder in Mode $06 before parts-cannoning.

dtcs.name: Cylinder 1 Misfire Detected
dtcs.description: Misfire isolated to cylinder 1. The PCM counts crankshaft speed variations attributable to cylinder 1's power stroke.

real_fixes.fix: Swap coil to another cylinder; if the misfire follows, replace the coil
Replace spark plug on cylinder 1
Replace cylinder 1 fuel injector after injector balance test

dtcs.tech_notes: Coil-swap test is the fastest isolation step. If plug and coil check out, run a relative compression test before pulling the head.

dtcs.name: Cylinder 2 Misfire Detected
dtcs.description: Misfire isolated to cylinder 2.

real_fixes.fix: Swap coil test, replace cylinder 2 coil if misfire follows
Replace spark plug

dtcs.tech_notes: Same isolation flow as P0301: coil swap, plug inspection, injector balance, relative compression.

dtcs.name: Cylinder 3 Misfire Detected
dtcs.description: Misfire isolated to cylinder 3.

real_fixes.fix: Swap coil test, replace cylinder 3 coil if misfire follows
Replace spark plug

dtcs.tech_notes: On transverse V6s cylinder 3 is often rear-bank; check intake plenum gaskets for vacuum leaks localized to that runner.

dtcs.name: Cylinder 4 Misfire Detected
dtcs.description: Misfire isolated to cylinder 4.

real_fixes.fix: Swap coil test, replace cylinder 4 coil if misfire follows
Replace spark plug
Cylinder leak-down test revealing burned exhaust valve, valve job

dtcs.tech_notes: If the misfire is only at idle and smooths out under load, suspect a small valve-seat issue; leak-down test confirms.

dtcs.name: Cylinder 6 Misfire Detected
dtcs.description: Misfire isolated to cylinder 6.

real_fixes.fix: Replace collapsed AFM lifter and check camshaft lobe (GM 5.3L V8)
real_fixes.frequency: most common verified fix on GM trucks

real_fixes.fix: Swap coil test, replace coil if misfire follows

dtcs.tech_notes: On 2014+ GM 5.3L, P0306 with a cold-start tick is the classic AFM lifter failure signature. Cylinders 1, 4, 6, 7 are the AFM cylinders.

dtcs.name: System Too Lean (Bank 1)
dtcs.description: Long-term fuel trim on bank 1 has exceeded the lean limit — the engine is getting more air or less fuel than the PCM expects.
common_causes: Vacuum leak (intake gaskets, PCV hoses, brake booster line)

real_fixes.fix: Smoke-test intake and repair vacuum leak
Clean or replace MAF sensor
Replace fuel pump after fuel-pressure and volume test

dtcs.tech_notes: Check fuel trims at idle vs 2500 RPM: lean at idle that improves with RPM points to a vacuum leak; lean everywhere points to MAF or fuel delivery.

dtcs.name: System Too Lean (Bank 2)
dtcs.description: Long-term fuel trim on bank 2 has exceeded the lean limit. When paired with P0171, the cause is common to both banks.
common_causes: Vacuum leak common to both banks

real_fixes.fix: Replace intake manifold gaskets (common on Ford 5.4L and GM V8s)
Clean or replace MAF sensor

dtcs.tech_notes: P0171 + P0174 together almost always rules out single-bank causes like one injector or one O2 sensor.

dtcs.name: System Too Rich (Bank 1)
dtcs.description: Long-term fuel trim on bank 1 has exceeded the rich limit — too much fuel or too little measured air.
common_causes: Saturated EVAP canister purging liquid fuel

real_fixes.fix: Replace leaking injector(s) found via injector balance test
Replace fuel pressure regulator after confirming high rail pressure

dtcs.tech_notes: Black, sooty plugs confirm a genuine rich condition versus a sensor-reporting issue.

dtcs.name: Catalyst System Efficiency Below Threshold (Bank 1)
dtcs.description: The downstream O2 sensor on bank 1 is switching too much, indicating the catalytic converter is no longer storing oxygen efficiently.
common_causes: Exhaust leak ahead of the downstream sensor
Unresolved misfire or rich condition poisoning the cat

real_fixes.fix: Replace bank 1 catalytic converter (OEM-grade; budget aftermarket cats frequently re-set the code)
Replace downstream O2 sensor
Repair exhaust leak at flex pipe or manifold gasket

dtcs.tech_notes: Fix any misfire or fuel-trim codes FIRST — a new cat will fail again if the upstream cause survives. Compare upstream vs downstream O2 waveforms: a healthy cat shows a flat downstream signal.

dtcs.name: Catalyst System Efficiency Below Threshold (Bank 2)
dtcs.description: Bank 2 equivalent of P0420 — catalyst oxygen storage below threshold on the bank opposite cylinder 1.

real_fixes.fix: Replace bank 2 catalytic converter
Replace bank 2 downstream O2 sensor

dtcs.tech_notes: Same discipline as P0420: confirm no misfire/fuel-trim codes first, and verify which bank is which before ordering parts.

dtcs.name: Evaporative Emission Control System Malfunction
dtcs.description: General EVAP system fault — the system failed its self-test but the PCM couldn't classify the leak size.

real_fixes.fix: Replace gas cap and clear code
Smoke-test EVAP system and repair leaking hose or solenoid

dtcs.tech_notes: Always smoke-test before replacing solenoids. See the EVAP smoke test procedure for port locations and PSI limits.

dtcs.name: EVAP System Leak Detected (Small Leak)
dtcs.description: The EVAP monitor detected a leak roughly equivalent to a 0.040 inch orifice.
common_causes: Cracked EVAP hose at a connector

real_fixes.fix: Replace gas cap
Smoke-test and replace cracked vapor hose
Replace purge solenoid that fails to hold vacuum

dtcs.tech_notes: Small leaks rarely show with soap spray — use a smoke machine at no more than 1 PSI.

dtcs.name: EVAP System Leak Detected (Large Leak)
dtcs.description: The EVAP monitor detected a gross leak — the system could not pull or hold any vacuum/pressure.
common_causes: Gas cap left off or cross-threaded

real_fixes.fix: Reseat or replace gas cap
Reconnect or replace disconnected vapor line (check after recent fuel pump or filler neck work)
Replace vent solenoid stuck open

dtcs.tech_notes: If the car was just in for fuel system work, check every line that was touched before anything else.

dtcs.name: EVAP System Leak Detected (Very Small Leak)
dtcs.description: Leak roughly equivalent to a 0.020 inch orifice — the hardest EVAP leak class to find.
common_causes: Leaking O-ring at purge valve or sensor

real_fixes.fix: Replace gas cap
Extended smoke test with shop lights off to spot wisps at hose unions

dtcs.tech_notes: Chrysler products set this code aggressively. Verify the leak standard with a calibrated 0.020 orifice tool before chasing ghosts.

dtcs.name: Exhaust Gas Recirculation Flow Insufficient
dtcs.description: The PCM commanded EGR flow and did not see the expected response (MAP change or EGR temp rise).

real_fixes.fix: Clean carbon from EGR passages and intake ports
Replace DPFE sensor (Ford trucks and vans)
Replace EGR valve

dtcs.tech_notes: On Hondas, the upper intake EGR port clogs at the manifold — there is a service kit with a drill-out procedure.

dtcs.name: Coolant Thermostat Below Regulating Temperature
dtcs.description: The engine took too long to reach operating temperature — coolant temp stayed below the thermostat's regulating threshold.

real_fixes.fix: Replace thermostat
Replace ECT sensor reading low

dtcs.tech_notes: Watch live ECT data on a cold start: it should climb steadily to ~195°F. A plateau in the 160s is a stuck-open thermostat.

dtcs.name: Mass Air Flow Circuit Range/Performance
dtcs.description: MAF sensor reading is implausible compared with throttle position, RPM, and MAP — reporting too much or too little air for conditions.
common_causes: Air leak between MAF and throttle body

real_fixes.fix: Clean MAF with dedicated MAF cleaner
Repair split or loose intake boot downstream of MAF
Replace MAF sensor

dtcs.tech_notes: At idle a healthy MAF reads roughly 1 g/s per liter of displacement. Check the intake boot with the engine torqued under load — cracks open up under movement.

dtcs.name: Mass Air Flow Circuit Low Input
dtcs.description: MAF signal voltage below expected range — near-zero airflow reported while the engine is running.

real_fixes.fix: Repair corroded MAF connector terminals
Replace MAF sensor

dtcs.tech_notes: Backprobe the signal wire: ~0.6-1.0V at idle on analog MAFs. Zero volts with good power and ground means the sensor is dead.

dtcs.name: Intake Air Temperature Sensor Circuit High Input
dtcs.description: IAT signal voltage stuck high, which the PCM reads as extremely cold intake air (often -40°).
common_causes: Open in signal or ground wire
Failed IAT element (often built into MAF)

real_fixes.fix: Reconnect or repair IAT/MAF connector
Replace MAF assembly containing the IAT element

dtcs.tech_notes: Jumper the two connector pins: if the scan tool reading swings to maximum hot, the wiring is fine and the sensor is open.

dtcs.name: Idle Air Control System RPM Lower Than Expected
dtcs.description: Actual idle RPM is below the PCM's target and the idle control system can't raise it.
common_causes: Excessive engine load from dragging AC compressor

real_fixes.fix: Clean throttle body and perform idle relearn
Replace IAC valve (cable-throttle engines)

dtcs.tech_notes: On electronic throttles, ALWAYS run the idle relearn procedure after cleaning — many makes will idle worse until relearned.

dtcs.name: Idle Air Control System RPM Higher Than Expected
dtcs.description: Actual idle RPM is above target — the engine is getting unmetered or uncommanded air.
common_causes: Failed idle relearn after battery disconnect or TB cleaning

real_fixes.fix: Perform throttle body idle relearn procedure
Smoke-test and fix vacuum leak

dtcs.tech_notes: Classic on Nissans after a battery disconnect — the pedal-dance idle relearn fixes it without parts.

dtcs.name: Transmission Control System Malfunction
dtcs.description: Informational code: the TCM has stored its own fault and asked the PCM to turn on the MIL. The real code lives in the TCM.
common_causes: Any TCM-detected fault — shift solenoids, pressure switches, internal failures

real_fixes.fix: Scan the TCM directly for its stored codes and diagnose those

dtcs.tech_notes: Never diagnose P0700 alone — it's the messenger. A generic OBD2 reader often can't see TCM codes; use an enhanced scan tool.

dtcs.name: Torque Converter Clutch Circuit Performance or Stuck Off
dtcs.description: The TCC was commanded to lock and slip stayed above threshold — the converter clutch isn't fully applying.

real_fixes.fix: Replace torque converter and flush cooler lines
Replace TCC solenoid
Fluid and filter service when fluid is burnt but slip is marginal

dtcs.tech_notes: Watch TCC slip RPM on the scan tool at steady 45 mph cruise: locked should read near zero. Honda V6s and Toyota 4-speeds are frequent fliers.

dtcs.name: Camshaft Position A Timing Over-Advanced (Bank 1)
dtcs.description: Actual intake cam position is more advanced than commanded, or stuck advanced — the VVT system can't hit target.
common_causes: Dirty or sludged oil starving the VVT actuator

real_fixes.fix: Oil and filter change with correct viscosity, clean OCV screen
Replace oil control valve / VVT solenoid
Replace cam phaser and timing chain set

dtcs.tech_notes: Ask about oil change history first — this code is the engine telling on its maintenance. Verify oil pressure before condemning phasers.

dtcs.name: Crankshaft/Camshaft Position Correlation (Bank 1 Sensor A)
dtcs.description: The crank and cam position signals are out of expected alignment — mechanical timing or a sensor/reluctor problem.
common_causes: Sheared reluctor or damaged tone ring
Wrong sensor air gap after repair

real_fixes.fix: Replace stretched timing chain, guides, and tensioner
Replace cam phaser that won't park at base position

dtcs.tech_notes: Scope crank vs cam signals and compare with a known-good capture before tearing down the front of the engine.

dtcs.name: Knock Sensor 1 Circuit Malfunction (Bank 1)
dtcs.description: Knock sensor signal out of range — the PCM may default to retarded timing, costing power and fuel economy.

real_fixes.fix: Replace knock sensor(s) and sub-harness (GM V8: under the intake manifold, replace both)
Repair corroded connector

dtcs.tech_notes: Knock sensors are torque-critical — overtightening skews the piezo response. Torque to spec, never guess.

dtcs.name: System Voltage Low
dtcs.description: The PCM observed system voltage below threshold while the engine was running — the charging system isn't keeping up.
common_causes: Parasitic draw dragging a weak battery down

real_fixes.fix: Replace alternator after confirming low charging voltage and ripple
Clean and retorque battery terminals and ground straps

dtcs.tech_notes: Check voltage at the battery AND at the PCM power feed — a half-volt drop across a corroded cable mimics a weak alternator.

dtcs.name: Lost Communication With ECM/PCM
dtcs.description: Another module on the CAN bus reports it cannot hear the engine controller. Often a no-start or stall with multiple modules complaining.
common_causes: PCM power or ground circuit failure
CAN bus wiring damage (rodents, chafe points)

real_fixes.fix: Repair chafed or rodent-damaged CAN wiring
Repair PCM power/ground feeds (blown fuse, corroded ground)
Replace and reprogram PCM

dtcs.tech_notes: Check which modules CAN'T be reached vs which can — the pattern maps where the bus is broken. 60-ohm resistance across CAN H/L with key off is the quick bus check.

dtcs.name: Lost Communication With ABS Control Module
dtcs.description: Modules on the bus can't hear the ABS controller. Usually accompanied by ABS and traction control lights.
common_causes: CAN wiring damage near the module

real_fixes.fix: Clean and repair ABS module connector and grounds
Replace ABS module (GM trucks: known solder-joint failures)

dtcs.tech_notes: ABS modules live in the splash zone — corroded connectors outnumber dead modules. Wiggle-test the harness while watching scan tool communication.

dtcs.name: Left Front Wheel Speed Sensor Circuit
dtcs.description: The ABS module sees an open, short, or implausible signal from the left front wheel speed sensor.
common_causes: Damaged sensor wiring at the strut flex point

real_fixes.fix: Replace wheel speed sensor
Replace hub/bearing assembly with integrated sensor or damaged tone ring
Repair harness chafe at the strut bracket

dtcs.tech_notes: Watch all four wheel speeds in live data while driving — a dropout that appears over bumps is wiring, a dead-zero is sensor or tone ring.

dtcs.name: Fuel Level Sensor Circuit High Input
dtcs.description: Fuel level signal above plausible range — gauge may peg full or swing erratically. Also blocks EVAP monitors from running.
common_causes: Worn sender card in the tank
Corroded connector at fuel pump module

real_fixes.fix: Replace fuel level sender or pump module assembly
Repair connector at the tank

dtcs.tech_notes: GM trucks wear the sender card at the half-tank spot — the gauge sticks where the truck spends its life. Sulfur-heavy fuel accelerates it.
